title: Leverage and the Cross-Section of Equity Returns
abstract: Building on theoretical asset pricing literature, we examine the role of market risk and the size, book-to-market (BTM), and volatility anomalies in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns. Compared with levered (stock) returns, unlevered market beta plays a more important role in explaining the cross-section of unlevered equity returns, even after controlling for size and BTM. The size effect is weakened, while the value premium and the volatility puzzle virtually disappear for unlevered returns. We show that leverage induces heteroskedasticity in returns. Unlevering returns removes this pattern, which is otherwise difficult to address by controlling for leverage in regressions.

article.introduction:
How does financial leverage affectthe cross-section of equity returns? Apart from being of intrinsic interest, this question is also relevant because of its relation to well-documented stylized facts on the cross-section of stock returns: the inability of the unconditional market model to explain the cross-section of stock returns (Fama and French (1992)) and the robust presence of a wide variety of anomalies. In this paper, we focus on unconditional market risk and three important anomalies: the size discount (Banz (1981), Fama and French (1992)), the value premium (Rosenberg, Reid, and Lanstein (1985), Fama and French (1992)),1and the negative cross-sectional relation between volatility and returns known as the volatility puzzle (Ang et al. (2006)). While there is widespread consensus on the existence of these anomalies, there is much less agreement on their economic interpretation.2In this paper, we investigate whether these empirical anomalies are due (in part) to financial leverage risk.
Our study differs from existing literature on the role of leverage in asset pricing because we develop and empirically test refutable hypotheses regarding the cross-section ofunleveredequity returns. In developing these testable hypotheses, we argue that for a large class of linear asset pricing models oflevered(stock) returns that are considered in the literature, systematic (levered) equity risk can be decomposed into systematic unlevered (or asset) risk and (possibly nonlinear) representations of leverage risk. This decomposition generates testable hypotheses for the cross-section ofunleveredreturns.
The most important testable hypothesis is that asset market beta should be priced in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns. With respect to size, various studies investigate its role in an economy without leverage. Real options models (Berk, Green, and Naik (1999), Carlson, Fisher, and Giammarino (2004)) predict that the size effect remains in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns. The robustness of the size effect, however, differs in these models. For example, in Berk, Green, and Naik (1999), it disappears under certain conditions (see the discussion in SectionI.Cbelow). Babenko, Boguth, and Tserlukevich (2016) argue that the size discount emerges if the empirical test design does not correctly account for all sources of priced risk.
Although the negative cross-sectional relation between volatility and returns is referred to as the volatility puzzle, an important class of models predicts a negative relation between volatility and levered equity returns. In contrast, these models do not impose restrictions on the relation of volatility to unlevered equity returns. For book-to-market (BTM), some models predict a positive relation between unlevered returns and BTM through theoperatingleverage channel, while other models derive BTM effects from risk related to financial leverage and therefore predict that BTM should not be priced in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns.
Our empirical investigation of the impact of leverage on stock returns is motivated by not only the stylized facts in the data, but also theory. We first analyze the empirical relation between leverage and stock returns. Consider Figure1, Panel A, which plots monthly stock returns against leverage in our sample.3The figure clearly indicates that leverage induces multiplicative heteroskedasticity (Harvey (1976)) in the data. We show that in many existing theoretical models, leverage induces such heteroskedasticity in the data.
It is well known that heteroskedasticity greatly complicates statistical inference. Addressing these issues by controlling for leverage in regressions is difficult because of the nonlinear patterns in the data that are evident from the figure. We therefore infer unlevered equity returns from observed stock returns on levered firms using a simple scaling suggested by the data as well as parametric models, such as the structural credit risk model of Merton (1974). We then run the asset pricing tests using the unlevered returns.4Figure1, Panel B, the unlevered counterpart to Panel A, demonstrates that unlevering returns effectively removes the nonlinear patterns in Panel A.
Our main empirical findings may be summarized as follows. Compared with the cross-section of levered equity returns, unlevered market beta plays a more important role—in terms of both its magnitude and statistical significance—in explaining the cross-section of unlevered equity returns, even when we control for size and BTM. In contrast, the size discount weakens and the value premium virtually disappears. Consistent with the theoretical predictions of structural credit risk models, we confirm the volatility puzzle for levered returns but not for unlevered returns. These findings suggest that size, BTM, and volatility affect the cross-section of stock returns in part through their relation with financial leverage–related risk.
These empirical results are robust. In particular, they hold when using univariate and bivariate portfolio sorts as well as Fama-MacBeth (1973) regressions, and they hold when using different structural credit risk models (e.g., Leland and Toft (1996)) and variations in empirical implementation. The existence of the value premium depends in part on the definition of leverage. Not surprisingly, when we use a more narrow definition of leverage, the results are more similar to those obtained for levered returns. In summary, our results point to a much more parsimonious factor model explaining the data: only the unlevered market beta and size are priced in the cross-section of unlevered returns—the value premium and volatility puzzle substantially weaken or disappear. This parsimony is desirable from a testing perspective (Lewellen, Nagel, and Shanken (2010)).
Our study is related to the extensive empirical literature that directly or indirectly suggests a role for financial leverage and distress in explaining the cross-sectional dispersion in expected stock returns.5Bhandari (1988) finds a positive relation between leverage and average stock returns. Fama and French (1992, p. 428) conclude that “the combination of size and BTM seem to absorb the role of leverage.” That is, size and BTM subsume not only systematic market risk but also leverage risk.6Choi (2013) also studies asset returns but focuses on the value premium and does not address the size discount, the volatility puzzle, or the cross-sectional performance of market beta. His focus is on conditional beta: during downturns, the asset beta and leverage of value firms increase, raising the equity beta, while the equity beta of growth firms remains relatively stable over the cycle.
The remainder of the paper is structured as follows. SectionIdevelops the implications of existing asset pricing models for the cross-section of unlevered returns. SectionIIdescribes the data. SectionIIIpresents empirical evidence on size, BTM, and asset beta using tests based on returns scaled by leverage. SectionIVreports empirical results for the cross-section of returns unlevered using the Merton (1974) model. SectionVdiscusses results of extensive robustness analyses, and SectionVIprovides evidence on the cross-sectional relation between volatility and returns. SectionVIIconcludes.
article.I. Theory and Testable Predictions:
Building on the theoretical asset pricing literature, we distinguish between theoretical predictions forunlevered equity(asset) returns andlevered equity(stock) returns. We differ from existing literature by formulating testable hypotheses for unlevered returns. We first outline the general framework and fix notation. Subsequently, we discuss testable hypotheses for several risk factors.
Consider a firm with market values of levered equity and debt given byEandD, respectively. Theasset(or unlevered equity) value of the firm is given byA. Leverage is denoted byL. Leverage may refer to eitherD/(D+E)orD/E, which will be indicated as needed.
The empirical literature on financial leverage typically decomposes the systematic risk of levered equityβLin terms of (1) the systematic risk of the underlying asset valueA, denoted byβA, and (2) the effect of leverage on shareholders' systematic risk, denoted by the functionΦ(L,δ,Z), whereZis a vector of leverage-related covariates other thanLandδthat may affect equity risk. That is,βLis represented by some functionalf(βA,Φ(L,δ,Z)). Note that because an unlevered firm is not exposed to leverage-related risk, we must haveΦ(L=0,δ,Z)=0.
As noted above, existing literature decomposes the systematic risk of stocks in terms of the asset (or unlevered) beta and systematic bankruptcy-related risk. Below, we discuss models of levered equity in the literature that use various parameterizations of Φ, as well as models of unlevered firms in whichΦ=0. All models we discuss share a common hypothesis, however, regarding the price of systematic asset risk in equation (2):
Hypothesis 1.The price of systematic asset risk is positive, that is,λA>0.

Empirical discussions of the size discount (Banz (1981), Fama and French (1992)) are based on levered returns. But should a size discount also obtain in unlevered returns? A growing number of papers incorporate capital investment and growth options in equilibrium asset pricing models to provide economic intuition for the size effect. Importantly, many of these models considerunleveredfirms and derive analytical representations of the firm's priced risk factors that include size. For instance, Carlson, Fisher, and Giammarino (2004) use the assumption of declining growth opportunities to argue that larger equity values (size) reflect a lower proportion of risky growth options, and hence imply a negative relation between size and expected returns for an unlevered firm. Berk, Green, and Naik (1999) show that, controlling for BTM, the firm's equity value (size) is a state variable that captures the relative importance of the firm's assets-in-place (AIP) and growth options and is negatively related to the expected equity return on an unlevered firm. The role of size as a risk factor is somewhat different here because the size discount disappears in the absence of dynamic variation in the relative importance of growth options, for example, when the firm has a single long-lived project.8Meanwhile, Babenko, Boguth, and Tserlukevich (2016) argue that a size discount emerges if all sources of priced risk are not correctly accounted for. The arguments in Babenko, Boguth, and Tserlukevich (2016) do not depend on leverage and therefore also apply to unlevered equity returns.
We conclude that existing theoretical literature predicts that the size effect remains in the cross-section of asset (unlevered equity) returns in equation2, although some equilibrium models and the factor mispricing argument of Babenko, Boguth, and Tserlukevich (2016) imply that size may not be priced for a subset of firms.
Hypothesis 2.The price of size in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns is negative, that is,γ2A<0.

Several strands of the theoretical literature make predictions regarding the BTM effect in unlevered and levered returns. We first consider the implications of real options models of unlevered firms. We then discuss models with financial leverage, including those with exogenous and strategic default.
In Carlson, Fisher, and Giammarino (2004), firms invest to exercise growth options and convert these options into AIP, which raises the firms' long-run operating leverage. During economic downturns or periods of low demand, the risks of AIP increase. The systematic risk of high BTM firms is therefore especially high because of their greater operating leverage. In a similar vein, Zhang (2005) models costly capital reversibility and a countercyclical price of risk to generate a greater systematic risk for high BTM firms, ceteris paribus. In Berk, Green, and Naik (1999), BTM is a state variable that, at any point in time, is positively related to the future systematic risk of the firm's assets. In Babenko, Boguth, and Tserlukevich (2016), idiosyncratic cash flows are negatively related to BTM and the firm's systematic risk. These models therefore predict a positive cross-sectional relation between BTM and unlevered equity returns, that is,γ1A>0.
In contrast, several models of equilibriumleveredreturns imply the absence of BTM effects in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns. Consider the widely used textbook treatment of asset and levered beta in a capital asset pricing model (CAPM) framework (Hamada (1972)), which posits thatβL=βA[1+(1−t)DE]. That is, in the notation of SectionI.A, the functionalf(·,·)is multiplicatively separable andΦ(L,δ,Z)=(1−t)DE, wheretis the marginal corporate income tax. In this case, leverage is the only risk factor besides asset beta that determines the levered beta, and hence BTM is not priced in the cross-section of unlevered returns.
While the textbook approach takes leverage to be fixed and does not consider financial distress costs, a more recent literature develops real options models for levered firms; these models make leverage endogenous and/or allow for financial distress costs. In several of these models (Gomes and Schmid (2010), Garlappi and Yan (2011)), BTM is related to leverage and financial distress costs and the levered beta is decomposed into asset beta and the bankruptcy-related systematic risk in a multiplicative fashion, that is,βL=βA[1+Φ(L,δ,Z)]. For our purposes, the most critical observation is that these predictions regarding the role of BTM are about the cross-section of levered returns. For unlevered returns, BTM is not priced.
In sum, based on the predictions from real option models of unlevered firms with dynamic asset composition as well as models of levered firms with financial distress costs, we conclude:
Hypothesis 3.The price of BTM in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns is nonnegative, that is,γ1A≥0.

Existing empirical literature examines the relation between levered equity (stock) returns and levered equity volatilityσLfollowing Ang et al. (2006), who document a negative cross-sectional relation between a stock's volatility and its subsequent return. More recent empirical literature investigates and largely confirms this cross-sectional relation (Chen et al. (2014)), which is often thought of as an anomaly and is referred to as the “volatility puzzle.”
The observed empirical relation seems inconsistent with the classical asset pricing view that only systematic risk should be priced in equilibrium. Theoretical deviations from this classical paradigm typically predict a positive relation between idiosyncratic volatility (IVOL) and expected returns (Merton (1987), Barberis and Huang (2001), Malkiel and Xu (2002)). Meanwhile, a growing literature provides various possible explanations for the negative IVOL-return relation (Chen et al. (2014), Stambaugh, Yu, and Yuan (2015), Babenko, Boguth, and Tserlukevich (2016), Herskovic et al. (2016)). It is important to note that these theoretical models do not explicitly consider leverage, and therefore the predictions also implicitly hold for unlevered returns.
We conclude that existing models generate very different hypotheses for the price of volatility in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns:
Hypothesis 4.Volatility in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns may not be priced, or may be positively or negatively priced. The sign ofγ3Ain equation(2)is therefore ambiguous(i.e.,γ3A⪋0).

In sum, we argue that empirically testable hypotheses from existing models regarding the cross-section of equity returns can usefully be expressed in terms of unlevered (asset) returns. It is critical that these hypotheses for unlevered returns (Hypotheses 1 to 4) are radically different from the stylized facts regarding the cross-section of levered equity returns, which we verify for our sample below. In the cross-section of levered returns, the stylized facts are thatβLis not priced, while BTM is positively priced and size and volatility are negatively priced. In contrast, for the cross-section of unlevered returns, theory predicts that the asset betaβAis positively priced. Except for a subset of firms, size is negatively priced. Some theories predict a positive relation between BTM and unlevered returns, while others predict no relation. With respect to volatility, different theories predict a positive, negative, or no relationship with unlevered equity returns.
article.II. Data:
We obtain stock returns and the number of shares outstanding from the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP). We denote the excess stock return (levered equity return) for firmiat timetbyRE,i(t). We limit attention to nonfinancial firms, consistent with Fama and French (1992). The risk-free rate comes from Kenneth French's website.
Throughout the paper, we use various measures of firm leverage to compute unlevered returns. These measures of leverage require information about the face value of the firm's debt. For most of our implementation, we proxy for the face value of the firm's debt using total liabilities. In robustness tests in SectionV, we provide results using total debt instead. We obtain quarterly data on the firm's debt and liabilities from Compustat.9Quarterly Compustat debt data are available as of 1971. Our sample period is thus from 1971 to 2012.
We first investigate the size and BTM anomalies in our sample of (levered) stock returns to ensure that the stylized facts of our sample are consistent with existing literature on the cross-section of stock returns. TableI, Panel A, reports stock returns for 25 size and BTM portfolios, computed according to Fama and French (1993). Consistent with the available literature, small firms and high BTM firms have higher stock returns in our sample. The average risk-free rate in the sample period is 0.43% per month. Hence, the excess returns for the 25 size and BTM portfolios are similar to the excess returns in Fama and French (1993), who report on a very different sample period. Panel A also reports the differences between the fifth and first size quintiles, conditional on BTM, and the differences between the fifth and first BTM quintiles, conditional on size. We also report thet-statistics for these differences. The differences between the fifth and first BTM quintiles are positive and economically large in all five cases, and they are statistically significant in three of the five cases.
Panel B of TableIcomplements the results in Panel A by reporting on univariate sorts on size and BTM. The signs on the long-short portfolios are consistent with existing literature, and as in Panel A, the statistical significance of the BTM anomaly is stronger than that of the size anomaly.
We next use Fama and French's (1992) regression approach and consider how size and BTM affect the cross-section of expected stock returns when they are taken to be firm characteristics (see Daniel and Titman (1997)). The results are reported in TableI, Panel C. We use as regressors the firm's levered market beta, the logarithm of the firm's market capitalizationln(E), the logarithm of book value over market valueln(BE/E), the logarithm of book value of assets over market value of equityln(BA/E), and the logarithm of book value of assets over book value of equityln(BA/BE). The results confirm the existence of two important anomalies for our sample. First, regardless of the other regressors, the coefficient onln(E)is significantly negative, confirming the size effect, whereas the coefficient onln(BE/E)is significantly positive, confirming the BTM effect; indeed, the size and BTM anomalies are statistically more significant compared to the sorting results in Panels A and B. Second, the levered market beta is not a priced risk factor when we control for size and BTM—it is not statistically significant and the estimate of the price of market risk has the wrong sign.
In sum, despite using a very different sample from Fama and French (1992), we confirm the statistical and economic significance of the size discount and the value premium in the cross-section of stock returns in our sample. We also confirm that the levered market beta is not significant when controlling for size and BTM.
If cross-sectional differences in leverage are related to the value premium but not the size discount, then we should find supportive evidence in leverage patterns. TableII, Panel A, presents average leverage, defined as the ratio of the book value of total liabilities to the sum of the book value of total liabilities and the market value of equity, for each of the 25 size and BTM portfolios. Panel B presents average leverage for univariate size and BTM quintile portfolios.
Both the univariate and the bivariate sorts indicate a strong positive relation between BTM and leverage.10Indeed, in Panel A, leverage increases with BTM in every size quintile, and this increase is monotonic within each size quintile. Moreover, the difference between the average leverage of the BTM 5 and BTM 1 quintiles is highly statistically significant for each size group. Because stock returns and financial leverage are positively related (Bhandari (1988)), this evidence provides support for the hypothesis that the positive BTM effect on stock returns is due to an underlying positive relation between BTM and leverage. In contrast, the relation between size and leverage is much less pronounced.
article.III. Leverage-Induced Heteroskedasticity:
In this section, we address the leverage-induced heteroskedasticity patterns in stock returns by simply scaling stock returns using leverage. This adjustment can be thought of as entirely data-driven, based on the pattern in Figure1, Panel A, which we highlighted in the introduction. For expositional ease, we henceforth refer to these returns as the “scaled returns.”
We investigate the role of the risk factors using the scaled returns. TableIIIillustrates the implications of unlevering. We simply scale excess levered equity returnsRE,i(t)by1−Li(t−1), whereLiis proxied by the ratio of the book value of total liabilities to the sum of the book value of total liabilities and the market value of equity. TableIII, Panels A and B, present the rescaled portfolio returns based on bivariate and univariate sorts. We present the average value-weighted returns for each of the size and BTM portfolios, where the weights are determined by the market value of levered equity, consistent with TableI. TableIII, Panel A, presents the results for the 25 size and BTM portfolios. The firms in each of the 25 cells are exactly the same ones as in Panel A of TableI.
The results are striking. The pattern of returns on unlevered equity as a function of BTM is markedly different from the pattern for levered stock returns shown in TableI, Panel A. In contrast to the monotone positive relation between stock returns and BTM, which constitutes the value premium, the relation between unlevered equity returns and BTM is complex and nonmonotone. Given the complex and polynomial relation between unlevered equity returns and BTM across all size groups, it is not surprising that the difference between the return on the highest and lowest BTM quintiles is not statistically significant for any size group. This is in contrast to stock returns, where the difference between the return on the highest and lowest BTM quintiles is positive and both statistically and economically significant for Size 1 through Size 3. Thus, we conclude that unlevering returns has a large impact on the BTM effect. The size effect observed for stock returns also appears in the scaled returns.
The univariate sorts in TableIII, Panel B, confirm the results from the bivariate sorts in Panel A. The magnitude of the long-short portfolio return associated with size is smaller than that in Panel B of TableI, and statistically less significant. The long-short portfolio return associated with BTM is much smaller compared to Panel B of TableI, switches sign, and is no longer statistically significant.11
In TableIII, Panel C, we run a Fama-MacBeth (1973) regression of the rescaledRE,i(t)(1−Li(t−1))on various permutations of market beta, size, and BTM. Note that once leverage is incorporated this way, there is no need to include it as a regressor, and market betas are recomputed using a market portfolio that aggregates the rescaled returns. In sharp contrast to the results in TableI, Panel C, the estimate of the price of market risk varies between 0.09% and 0.13% per month. The results for size and BTM in Panel C are qualitatively consistent with the bivariate and univariate sorts in Panels A and B. The size effect is economically smaller and statistically less significant compared to the results for levered returns. The BTM effect is no longer statistically significant in TableIII, Panel C.
While correcting for leverage strengthens the evidence in favor of the market model, the estimate of the price of risk is lower than the historical sample average of the market risk premium of 0.29% and the estimates are not statistically significant at conventional significance levels. Thus, in the last row of Panel C, we repeat the test while imposing the sample average for the market risk premium. The results show that this does not affect our conclusions regarding size and BTM.
We now provide additional evidence on the performance of the market model. Recall that we follow the implementation of Fama and French (1992), who group the stocks into 100 portfolios and compute market betas for these portfolios. Figure2depicts the results of a cross-sectional regression of the average return for these portfolios, rather than the individual firms, and the portfolio betas. Figure2, Panel A, reports on levered returns, and Panel B on scaled returns. The relation between market beta and returns in Panel A appears to be noisier than that in Panel B, especially for high betas. The resulting differences in the estimates of the market risk premium are much more pronounced than the differences based on individual firms in TableIII. For the levered returns in Panel A, the estimate of the market risk premium is 0.05% per month with at-statistic of 0.80. For the scaled returns in Panel B, the estimate of the price of risk is 0.11 with at-statistic of 3.95. Note that this estimate of the price of risk is very similar to the estimate in TableIII. Thet-statistic cannot be directly compared to thet-statistic in TableIII, Panel C, which is based on Fama-MacBeth (1973) regressions. However, comparing the results in Panels A and B of Figure2shows that unlevering the portfolio returns provides estimates of the price of market risk that are economically and statistically more significant.
We conclude that market beta performs much better in explaining the cross-sectional variation of unlevered returns compared with the cross-section of levered returns, especially when using portfolios. What drives these results? One possible explanation is that the distribution of unlevered returns conforms more closely to the sufficient conditions for the CAPM, for example, with respect to the higher moments. In the Internet Appendix, we therefore examine the properties of the distribution of levered and unlevered returns. However, the differences in the higher moments (between the two distributions) are rather small. An alternative explanation is that unlevered returns conform more to the econometric properties needed to reliably estimate the model. These improvements are more obvious when using portfolio returns in Figure2rather than firm-level returns because the differences between levered and scaled returns are more pronounced due to the absence of noise. Of course, despite these findings, the significance of size means that the market model is rejected.
Our findings on market risk are meaningful for the large literature (Fama and French (1992) and onward) on the role of market beta in the cross-section of stock returns. A number of papers argue that time-varying (or conditional) market betas and risk premia perform well in explaining the cross-sectional variation of stock returns (see, for example, Jagannathan and Wang (1996)). However, Nagel and Singleton (2011) question whether the proposed conditional models are a good fit for the data. Our analysis shows that theunconditionalunlevered (or asset) market beta performs better in explaining the cross-section of unlevered returns than is commonly thought, even when controlling for size and BTM. As discussed in SectionI, the positive relation between expected unlevered equity returns and systematic asset risk is a common feature of a large number of models, including real options models.
In summary, the principal results of this section are as follows. Consistent with theory reviewed in SectionI(Hypothesis 1), theunleveredmarket beta is priced in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns, even when we control for size and BTM. The estimates of the market risk premium are plausible but not statistically significant. Furthermore, there is a negative relation between size and unlevered returns, but it is economically and statistically weaker than for levered returns. Finally, the zero price for BTM in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns is consistent with theoretical models that interpret BTM effects as arising from financial leverage and the costs of financial distress (Hypothesis 3).12These findings are very different from the results obtained for levered returns.
article.IV. Unlevering Stock Returns with a Para:
Our approach in SectionIIIis motivated by the patterns in the data, starting from the observation that stock returns display heteroskedasticity when viewing the returns as a function of leverage. In this section, we start from theory instead. We consider the possibility that the patterns in stock returns are consistent with economic theory, and use theory to transform the data into unlevered returns. Specifically, we unlever stock returns using the Merton (1974) model, and present results for asset betaβA,BTM, and size (E) using the unlevered returns. We then discuss how these results differ from those in TableIIIand from the results for levered stock returns in TableI.
Finally, we show that adding leverage to regressions with levered returns yields very different results. This finding explains why prior literature has not uncovered the importance of leverage for the anomalies we study in this paper.
We proceed by inferring unlevered returns from levered (or stock) returns for all firms in our sample and subsequently conduct empirical tests using the unlevered returns. In principle, we can unlever stock returns using any model discussed in SectionIthat explicitly describes the mapping from unlevered to levered returns, such as Hamada (1972), Garlappi and Yan (2011), and Gomes and Schmid (2010). We use Merton's (1974) structural credit risk model, which provides several advantages. Within the class of models that allow for a nonlinear relation between stock returns and leverage, the Merton (1974) model is the simplest. This provides expositional convenience and facilitates intuition regarding our empirical results. Because it is the most parsimonious model in this class, we also avoid additional ad hoc assumptions regarding ancillary parameters. Finally, the Merton (1974) model has been widely studied in the credit risk literature, and as a result, existing literature is quite instructive regarding robust implementation of this model.
Figure3, Panel A, illustrates this relation for three different values of the volatility of unlevered firm equityσA. On the horizontal axis, we have the ratio of the market value of the debtDand the market value of the firm's equityE. We assume that the yearly expected return on the unlevered firm equityμAis 6%. The risk-free rate is assumed to be 3%, and the initial value of the unlevered firm is assumed to be 100. Given the 6% expected return on unlevered equity, the expected value of the unlevered firm after one month is equal to 100.5. We compute the initial levered equity value and the levered equity value after one month for different face values of debt and different values of unlevered equity volatility (UEV). On the vertical axis, we have the expected excess return on the levered firm's equityμL−r, which is concave in theD/Eratio.
For our purposes, it is useful to consider Figure3, Panel B, which graphs expected excess equity returns as a function of leverage, defined asD/(D+E), which by definition is bounded between zero and one. The difference between levered and unlevered equity returns increases with leverage, as expected. Moreover, at high levels of leverage, the relationship between leverage and expected stock returns becomes highly convex for realistic values of UEVσA. When adjusting firms' equity returns for leverage, it is important to take these nonlinearities into account. This may be especially relevant to understanding the returns of firms with high BTM because we will show below that these firms typically are also more highly levered.
Note that when the firm's unlevered realized equity return is negative, leverage once again amplifies this effect. However, this now means that levered returns become more negative and thusdecline. Expected returns are positive in equilibrium, but in empirical tests, ex-post returns are used as proxies for expected returns, and these are frequently negative. We conclude that the sign of the unlevered equity return determines the implications of leverage for levered returns. This is illustrated in Panel C of Figure3.
The approach in SectionIIIis data-driven and starts from the empirical observation of heteroskedasticity. In contrast, using the Merton (1974) model to unlever returns starts from economic theory. We now argue that these two approaches are, in fact, closely related. We demonstrate this in two ways.
First, the results in TableIIIrescale excess levered equity returnsRE,i(t)by1−Li(t−1)based on the empirical pattern in the data. However, this approach can be thought of as the application of a model to unlever returns. Recall from SectionIthatβL=βA[1+Φ(L,δ,Z)], where in the textbook treatment of asset and levered beta in a CAPM framework (Hamada (1972)), we haveΦ(L,δ,Z)=(1−t)DE. Therefore, ignoring taxes, our rescaling of the levered equity return, which is motivated by econometric concerns, is consistent with unlevering according to the textbook treatment of leverage, because1+Φ(L,δ,Z)=1+DE=D+EE.
Now consider equation5. Ignoring the required excess debt returnμD−r, we obtainμA−r=(μL−r)EA. Therefore, when we multiplyREby1−Lin SectionIII, this is conceptually equivalent to the use of equations4and5, but it ignores one component of the weighted cost of capital and focuses only on equity. With respect to the data used, when adjustingREby1−Lin SectionIII, we proxy forEusing the observed market value of equity, which is what the existing literature does when implementing the Merton (1974) model in equation4. However, when implementing equation4, the value ofDis the market value as computed from the Merton (1974) model. In SectionIII, no model is involved and the value ofDis the book value of total liabilities from Compustat.
To further illustrate the relation between these two approaches, we also report on an intermediary case. Note that when implementing the Merton (1974) model, the face value of the debt is a feature of the implied option, but leverage itself is computed using the market value of the debt as implied by the model. We therefore use an intermediary case whereμA−r=(μL−r)EA, butA=E+Dis computed using market debt according to the Merton (1974) model. This implementation is interesting from two perspectives. First, it is identical to the approach in SectionIIIexcept for the computation of the debtDbecause we use the market value of debt implied by the Merton (1974) model. Second, it amounts to using equation4with the derivative∂E∂Aequal to one.
These arguments also suggest that stock returns exhibit heteroskedasticity in a variety of models. Figures1and3use the Merton (1974) model to illustrate the implications of leverage, but similar results will obtain for most reasonable models of leverage. The exact nonlinear relation depends, of course, on the structure and parameterization of the Merton (1974) model and will differ somewhat from other models, but the emergence of the heteroskedasticity pattern is a general result.
To further illustrate the implications of leverage and the relation with heteroskedasticity, in the Internet Appendix (Figure IA.1), we show that simulated data from the Merton (1974) model can exhibit the heteroskedasticity observed in the data, confirming that unlevering returns using a parametric model and adjusting them by1−Lare closely related. Unlevering the data with the Merton (1974) model or another reasonable parametric model implicitly addresses heteroskedasticity in the data due to leverage.
Another perspective on the relation between the simple scaled returns used in SectionIIIand the use of parametric models is that it represents the classical trade-off between bias and efficiency. The latter approach uses a model to unlever stock returns and introduces biases if the model is incorrectly specified. Because the former approach does not use a model, it circumvents this problem. However, while it makes fewer assumptions, it may be less efficient than unlevering using a parametric model if the model is correctly specified.
TableIVreports on asset beta, size, and BTM in the cross-section of equity returns unlevered using the Merton (1974) model.16Panels A and C report on unlevered returns obtained using equation4. Panels B and D report on returns unlevered by scalingRE,i(t)by1−Li(t−1), whereLi(t−1)is computed using the market value of equity and the market value of debt computed using the Merton (1974) model, as discussed in SectionIV.B. Panels A and B report on portfolio sorts. Panels C and D report on Fama-MacBeth (1973) regressions.
Panels A and B of TableIVpresent average returns on unlevered equity using univariate sorts on size and BTM. These results can be compared with the sorts from TableIIIand the results for levered returns from TableI. Following Eom, Helwege, and Huang (2004), the face value of debt in the Merton (1974) model is assumed to be equal to the total liabilities from Compustat. The maturity of the debt is assumed to be 3.38 years, which is the average debt maturity obtained in Stohs and Mauer (1996) for a smaller sample. In robustness tests in SectionV, we provide results for alternative definitions of firm debt and debt maturity. We conclude that the empirical results in Panels A and B are very similar and that there are only minor differences with the results in TableIII. Compared with levered returns, the size effect is smaller and less significant. The BTM effect is economically small and statistically insignificant. This result for BTM is very different from the results in TableIfor levered returns.
Panels C and D of TableIVpresent the results of Fama-MacBeth (1973) regressions. The results are largely consistent with the results in Panel C of TableIII. In sharp contrast to the results in TableI, Panel C, the coefficient on asset beta is positive even in the presence of size and BTM. However, as in TableIII, the estimate is not statistically significant at conventional significance levels. The size effect survives but is smaller and less significant compared to the case of levered returns in TableI. This is again consistent with the evidence in TableIII. Finally, the evidence on BTM again contrasts with the findings for levered returns in TableI—the estimate is positive but statistically insignificant, and is economically much smaller, consistent with the sorts in Panels A and B. These findings are qualitatively similar to the results in TableIII.
Panels C and D of Figure2depict the cross-sectional relation between market beta and average unlevered returns at the portfolio level. The results confirm those from Panel B obtained using the scaled returns. The relation between market beta and returns in Panel A appears to be noisier than those in Panels C and D. Estimates of the market risk premium are much larger than those obtained in Panel A for levered returns and are statistically very significant. These findings again suggest that estimation of the market price of risk may be more reliable when using unlevered returns.
We conclude that these findings confirm the results from TableIII. By explicitly unlevering returns based on a parametric model, these results highlight the relevance of our results for the sources of the BTM effect. The literature proposes several explanations for the value premium that interpret it either as a rational reward for risk or evidence of irrational investor behavior. Because leverage represents risk in the Merton (1974) model, our results suggest that the value premium is a reward for risk.
In summary, the results of this section are in line with the results from SectionIII. Consistent with theory reviewed in SectionI(Hypothesis 1), theunleveredmarket beta is priced in the cross-section of unlevered equity returns even when we control for size and BTM. The estimates of the market risk premium are much larger than in existing literature—they are intuitively plausible but still smaller than the historical average of the excess return. There is a negative relation between size and unlevered returns (Hypothesis 2) but weaker evidence for a BTM effect (Hypothesis 3).
These findings raise the question of why the importance of leverage for the BTM anomaly has not been uncovered in existing work that includes leverage as a covariate.17We address this question in two ways. First, rather than analyze this issue using the return data in our sample, for which we do not know the data-generating process, we use a controlled experiment. TableV, Panel A, is based on return data simulated using the Merton (1974) model. The values of the model parameters used in the simulations arer=3%andT=3.38years. VolatilityσAis chosen to be uniformly distributed between 10% and 150%. DriftμAis chosen to be uniformly distributed between −6% and 6%. Leverage, defined as the ratio of book debt to asset value, is chosen to be uniformly distributed between zero and one. We simulate 10,000 monthly equity returns using these parameters and regress the simulated returns on leverage and higher order terms of leverage.
An additional advantage of simulated data is that we can keep the analysis simple because we do not have to consider additional explanatory variables, such as size and BTM. The data are generated using the Merton (1974) model, and we thus know that leverage should be significant in these regressions. TableV, Panel A, reports on regressions using the simulated data and linear as well as higher order terms in leverage. Leverage is not significant, regardless of whether higher order terms in leverage are included. The point estimates of the leverage terms are not statistically significant and theR2s are very small. The reason for this is that the relation between leverage and stock returns depends on the sign of the asset risk premium, as emphasized by Figure1.
Note that it is possible to extend this simulation exercise to incorporate market risk, which would allow us to investigate whether addressing heteroskedasticity allows us to retrieve this factor structure. We leave this exercise, which requires additional assumptions, for future work.
Panel B of TableVuses the data in our sample rather than simulated data. We include polynomials of leverage in Fama-MacBeth (1973) regressions with market betas, size, and BTM. The first row repeats the results from the last row of TableI, Panel C, which illustrates the size and BTM anomalies. Rows 2 through 4 of Panel A include linear, quadratic, and cubic leverage terms into the regression. Leverage is often insignificantly estimated. Consistent with existing literature, including leverage has little effect on the point estimates or on the statistical significance of market beta and the size and BTM characteristics.
article.V. Robustness:
In this section, we investigate the robustness of our empirical results along several dimensions. The empirical results in SectionIVare obtained using the Merton (1974) model. Implementing this model requires several assumptions, and we verify how these assumptions affect our results. First, we consider different assumptions on debt maturity. Second, we discuss results for different measures of the face value of the firm's debt. Third, we present results for the Leland and Toft (1996) model, which has a richer structure, to investigate whether our results are due to the use of the Merton (1974) model. Fourth, we use alternative weights. Fifth, we report on the robustness of the effect of leverage over time. Finally, we use guidance from the Merton (1974) model to scale returns in a more sophisticated way. In the Internet Appendix, we also discuss how results change when we restrict the sample based on leverage.
Structural credit risk models can be implemented with relatively few assumptions, and we use the classical Merton (1974) model because it uses as few assumptions as possible. One assumption is on the maturity of the debt. Recall that in our benchmark implementation in TableIV, we use the average maturity from Stohs and Mauer (1996), which is 3.38 years. Much of the existing literature on credit risk focuses on default prediction and therefore uses a maturity of one or five years. It is not clear if this is appropriate for our purposes, but we use these alternative assumptions to investigate how maturity impacts our results.
We conclude that debt maturity has a relatively small effect on the cross-sectional return patterns as a function of BTM and size or on the inference regarding market risk.
In our benchmark implementation of the Merton (1974) model, we use total liabilities as our proxy for the face value of the debt. Eom, Helwege, and Huang (2004) show that this measure yields more realistic spreads. In rows 5 and 6 of TableVI, we report on unlevered returns computed using an alternative definition of the face value of debt. Rather than using total liabilities as in TableIV, we now use total debt instead. Row 5 adjusts stock returns by1−L, while row 6 uses equation4instead. This assumption clearly affects our empirical results. The evidence on the size effect in Panel B does not change much, but the BTM effect in Panel A is economically and statistically larger compared to TableIV, although it is smaller compared to the effect for stock returns in TableI.
The Fama-MacBeth (1973) regressions in Panel C confirm these results. The size effect survives. For the BTM effect, the estimates are economically smaller and statistically less significant compared to TableI, but larger than in TablesIIIandIV. The economic magnitude and statistical significance for the price of market risk are very similar. These findings may be due to the fact that a more narrow definition of the face value of debt reduces the effects of leverage on unlevered returns. Therefore, the resulting empirical results for BTM are closer to the results for levered returns.
The results in row 7 of Panels A, B, and C indicate that the choice of model does not drive our results. The results for the size effect, BTM, and market beta are qualitatively similar to those in TableIV.
In row 8 of TableVI, we report alternative results where the unlevered equity returns are computed using different weights. In TableIV, portfolio unlevered equity returns are computed using the weights used in Panel A of TableI, which are based on (levered) stock market value. It may be preferable to use weights based on the value of unlevered equity, as some of the stock-based weights may be affected by leverage. Row 8 indicates that the effect on our results is minor.
Does the effect of leverage on stock returns change over time? Figure4addresses this question by graphing the cumulative long-short return for levered and unlevered equity over the sample, starting with a $1 investment in 1971.
Panel A graphs the cumulative returns for long-short BTM portfolios. It is well known that the return changes over time and that this time variation is related to the business cycle. We use the long-short unlevered returns from TableIII, Panel B. The figure indicates that the absence of a BTM effect in unlevered equity returns is not due to a small part of the sample period, but there is substantial variation in the long-short return on unlevered equity. Panel B presents the long-short returns for size portfolios and Panel C presents the long-short returns obtained by sorting on leverage. The differences between the two cumulative return series in Panel C are striking.
As we note in SectionIII, TableIIIpresents results using the simplest possible approach to scale stock returns using leverage. TableVIIpresents results for more sophisticated approaches. These approaches are motivated by the implications of the Merton (1974) model in equation4, and therefore further highlight the relation between scaling stock returns and unlevering stock returns using a parametric model.
The first row repeats the results from the last row of TableIII, Panel C, obtained by regressing the rescaled returnRE,i(t)(1−Li(t−1))on market beta, size, and BTM. In rows 2 through 4, we scale the levered equity return using increasingly sophisticated factors suggested by structural credit risk models. Note that the increased sophistication of these approaches comes at the cost that, unlike in row 1, the rescaling in rows 2 through 4 involves ancillary parameters that need to be estimated in a first step.
TableVIIindicates that the results are highly robust across the different implementations. We conclude that multiplicative heteroskedasticity is a serious problem when incorporating leverage into cross-sectional regressions of stock returns. When using scaled returns to remove the effects of leverage, the size effect remains but weakens economically and statistically, the BTM anomaly disappears, and market beta plays a more prominent role in explaining the cross-section of returns. Thet-statistics for the estimates of the price of market risk are somewhat higher than in TablesIIIandIV. It is possible that the regression approach reduces the noise in the adjusted returns.
article.VI. Volatility in the Cross-Section of E:
As noted in SectionI, existing asset pricing models impose different restrictions on the relation between unlevered volatility onunleveredequity returns. Here, we empirically investigate the role of volatility in the cross-section of equity returns. We first highlight differences between asset volatility and stock volatility. We then document the cross-sectional relation between volatility and levered as well as unlevered returns. We adjust for leverage using the regression specification in equation7. Asset volatility is computed as levered equity volatility times(1−Li(t−1)), whereLi(t−1)is implemented as in SectionIII.
Panel A of TableVIIIpresents descriptive statistics for stock volatility (EV) and UEV. There are clearly important differences. For instance, UEV is on average 17.3% lower than stock volatility. Panel B reports average leverage for portfolios sorted on volatility. The leverage spread is much higher in the case of UEV. These findings confirm existing results in the literature. For instance, Choi and Richardson (2016) emphasize the differences between stock volatility and asset volatility, and the critical role that leverage plays in explaining these differences.
As in SectionIII, we first analyze the relation between volatility and stock returns. Panel C of TableVIIIreplicates the results of Ang et al. (2006) for our sample, sorting onleveredequity volatility. We confirm the negative relation documented by Ang et al. (2006) using a univariate sort.
Panel D of TableVIIIreports on Fama-MacBeth (1973) regressions that include volatility as well as beta, size, and BTM. Levered volatility is estimated with a negative sign but is not statistically significant. Note that most existing empirical evidence points to a negative sign, consistent with Ang et al. (2006), but the evidence is not unequivocal. For example, Bali and Cakici (2008) find no robust relation between volatility and returns, while Boehme et al. (2009) document a positive volatility-return relation for small firms with nonbinding shorting constraints.19Compared to Panel C of TableI, the size effect is smaller and less statistically significant. The BTM effect remains strong both economically and statistically.
Panel E of TableVIIIreports on a univariate volatility sort and Panel F reports on Fama-MacBeth (1973) regressions. In Panel E, the unlevered equity return increases monotonically with UEV for the first four quintiles but strongly declines from the fourth to the fifth quintile. The long-short portfolio return spread is negative but much smaller than for levered returns and not statistically significant. The Fama-MacBeth (1973) regressions in Panel F report a positive but statistically insignificant loading on volatility. In sum, we can reject with a high degree of confidence the null hypothesis of a significantnegativerelation between unlevered equity returns and unlevered volatility.
We conclude that the negative cross-sectional relation between levered volatility and stock returns reported in the literature may be due to leverage, consistent with the predictions of structural credit risk models.
article.VII. Summary and Conclusions:
The effect of financial leverage on the cross-section of stock returns is an important question that remains unresolved in the literature. We build on the existing theoretical asset pricing literature to develop and empirically test refutable hypotheses regarding the cross-section of unlevered equity returns. We adjust observed stock returns for leverage and investigate the role of unlevered market betas and three important anomalies: size, BTM, and volatility. This approach is particularly useful because many asset pricing theories formulate testable hypotheses in terms of unlevered returns.
Identifying the relation between leverage and stock returns is challenging because it is highly nonlinear. We address the presence of leverage in returns in two ways. Our first approach is motivated by the empirical relation between stock returns and leverage. We scale returns using the leverage ratio. Our second approach unlevers returns using a parametric model. We show that these two approaches are related.
Although our sample period is different from the one used by Fama and French (1992,1993), we confirm the existence of the size effect, the volatility puzzle, and the value premium in levered returns and find that the levered market beta is not priced. Our analysis of unlevered returns yields two main results. First, a relatively parsimonious model explains the data: the value premium and volatility puzzle substantially weaken or disappear. Size is priced in the cross-section of unlevered returns but weakens economically and statistically. The estimates of the market risk premium are intuitively plausible. Second, leverage induces heteroskedasticity in the data. This implies that modeling the nonlinear relation between leverage and stock returns is challenging. Instead, we unlever stock returns prior to investigating the cross-section of returns.
We discuss the implications of these findings for existing theories. From the perspective of developing future theories for unlevered returns, our results suggest that they should incorporate the market model and allow for the presence of a size effect.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
